content:
The **confusion matrix** grew from **contingency tables** in **statistics** and **psychological testing** — cross-tabulating **stimulus present/absent** against **observer response yes/no**. Early **signal detection** work (1940s–1960s) used the same 2×2 structure for **hits, false alarms, misses, and correct rejections** before ROC curves plotted the trade-off across sensitivity settings.

**Medical diagnostics** adopted **sensitivity** (TPR) and **specificity** (TNR) from these tables long before machine learning — reporting **true/false positives and negatives** for screening tests is standard clinical practice.

As **statistical pattern recognition** and **expert systems** matured (1970s–1980s), ML papers tabulated **predicted vs actual class** counts for rule-based and neural classifiers.

The **scikit-learn** era (2010s) standardized `confusion_matrix`, `classification_report`, and heatmap displays — making the 2×2 (and K×K) table the default **post-threshold** evaluation artifact alongside threshold-free **ROC-AUC**. Modern ML ops still publishes confusion matrices at **deployment cutoffs** even when model selection uses ranking metrics.

timeline.label: Contingency tables in statistics (early 1900s)
timeline.description: Cross-classification of categorical outcomes — foundation for 2×2 error tables.

timeline.label: Signal detection hits & false alarms (1940s–60s)
timeline.description: Detection theory names the four outcomes that become TP, FP, TN, FN in binary classification.

timeline.label: Clinical sensitivity & specificity
timeline.description: Medical testing standardizes reporting from confusion-style counts before ML adoption.

timeline.label: ML libraries & MLOps (2000s–present)
timeline.description: sklearn confusion_matrix and dashboards make the table default for threshold-fixed classifier evaluation.
